channel.title: Comments for page "Good As New"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Good As New"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recognize that this is the beginning of a series, but it feels like it works as a little horror stand-alone. My only criticism is along the line of mlister's, namely that it could have stood to have a bit more breathing room, particularly between the installation and rejection. Still, very enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I never really put a ton of thought into that. It was mostly to drive people from one article of mine to the next before I had even of the canon.

A good chunk of the articles and tales in TTP were written out of order chronologically with respect to the timeline of that canon and were given links to references that were being made. For example, I wrote Products long before this tale, were the first of the Sakers were introduced, and thus have that link there. If I had known at the time of writing that TTP would congeal into what it was today, I probably would have been a little more economical with the links.

At some point down the line, I should probably clean that up to give it all more of a consolidated feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is visceral. While I can agree with some of mlister's criticism (with respect to the character dialogue feeling unnatural), the body horror juxtaposed with the cold Anderson representative's sterile characterization gets me feeling thoroughly unsettled all around. It's an easy +1.

That said, I'm currently reading through all of Twisted Pines and I was wondering: why do some crosslinks lead to Tales and SCPs that take place chronologically after this one? It doesn't make sense in the context of a standalone piece, nor does it make sense in the canon's wider chronology. Shouldn't articles set in the future backlink to this one, instead of the other way around? I've noticed this in a lot of the eatlier articles, too. This doesn't affect my enjoyment of these articles in the slightest; it just seems unconventional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pacing feels rushed, with there not being enough breathing room for any sense of permanence. Besides that, the dialogue doesn't sound like real speech, being unrealistic or even prose-like at times. I can't imagine an eleven year old using the word "tendrils" while he's in intense pain, or belting out a metaphor involving broken toys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Terrible customer service. I hate companies like these. It's a known problem with their stuff and they just leave the customer in shit like that. Hate it when that happens.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Hey Friends,

Just popping by to say that if you like this tale, the great Randomini did an audio adaptation as part of his 2015 Advent Calendar Event. The audio recording for Good As New was for December 17th. Check it out, and while you are there, check out the rest of Randomini's audio adaptations. You will not be disappointed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. Basically when ever Anderson needs to set up a sale they create a Saker unit to act as the intermediary (unless the sale is with a big patron, as was the case in Hunting Anderson.) I thought having the Saker units all call themselves Mr/Mrs/Ms Saker was neat, hence the naming scheme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Swinging by to say I fixed what I believe to have been a minor typo.

Also, I had no idea this was connected to the Peregrine stuff and the Saker stuff and all that. My upvote's enthusiasm is appropriately increased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah yes, my favorite measure of success: How many people I can have rant about the fate of my fictional characters in the comments.

Glad you liked the tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is this not rated higher? And wtf Anderson, you just give up and say with he'll get used to it or…? Really, no counseling or any attempts at anything like that? Even as a pure business venture, if he does nothing about the situation, does he think the kid's uncle will ever do business with him again?

…and all the +1s for making me rant about the future of a fictional boy. Well done.

item.title: Nice
item.content:encoded:
The ending is nicely done here.

The theme is similar to a game called:
Deus Ex: Human Revolution

item.title: Re: It didn't feel like it ended.
(no title)
item.content:encoded: Good fridge horror at the end there. :D

item.title: Re: It didn't feel like it ended.
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Am I getting it right that her implication was, either the prosthetic will synch up with him, and the problem will cease, or it will entirely grow over and take control, making the problem moot again?


Either way, upvoting.

item.title: Re: It didn't feel like it ended.
item.content:encoded:
The conclusion is subtle, but it's there if you pay close attention.

In fact, this is one of the best uses of subtle fill-in-the-blanks horror I've seen on the site in a long while.

item.title: It didn't feel like it ended.
item.content:encoded: I was immersed within the reading until when I reached the ending, where it just abruptly ended. I felt as though the story did not progress or reach a conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There were some grammar slip ups ("there has been an accident") and parts of the syntax bugged me ("a heavy piece of gauze… A heavy bandage…" Using the word "heavy" twice in two back-to-back sentences reads a bit awkwardly) and the part where the father seems only to be mildly exasperated/counciling after his son tried to saw his own arm off kind of drew me out of the story (Yeah I know he screamed "Jesus Christ" but after that he didn't seem as concerned as a father would be in that situation… Like I don't think that he'd be muttering "Oh buddy…" and all that) but aside from these minor quibbles I enjoyed it enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was the saddest thing I've read in a very long time. +1.

item.title: Opening Post
item.content:encoded:
Alright guys.

Thought I'd try to contribute one more time before classes pick up again at the end of the month.

Special thanks to Twisted Gears, Devereaux, djkaktus, Silber and Dr. Leonard for helping with the draft of this.

For those curious, the Anderson mentioned in this tale is the same Anderson from SCP-1360 and the Peregrine tale series.

Hope you enjoy.

JC
